title: Hazelnut roulade
subtitle: Flourless hazelnut sponge rolled around whipped cream, served in thick slices.
excerpt: Hazelnut roulade is a flourless nut sponge that is rolled when warm, filled with whipped cream when cool, and served in generous slices. The sponge is light and slightly chewy rather than springy, and it cracks as it rolls; this is expected and part of the look. The whole thing takes about an hour from start to finish.
sourceNotes: Nut-based roulades without flour became popular in British cooking from the 1970s onwards, partly through the influence of American Jewish baking traditions where flourless cakes were required for Passover. The Roulade format, rolling a flat baked sponge around a filling, has French origins in the bûche de Noël tradition. Hazelnut roulades specifically appear in British recipe books from the 1980s and 1990s; Nigella Lawson and Jane Grigson both published versions. This recipe follows the standard method: beaten egg whites folded into yolk-and-sugar base, baked quickly at high heat, rolled while warm.

content.text: Hazelnut roulade is a flourless sponge that depends entirely on beaten eggs for its structure. There is no gluten to hold it together, which makes it naturally tender and slightly fragile. It will crack when you roll it, and that is fine: the crack runs lengthways along the top and becomes part of the look once it is filled and sliced.

Roll the sponge while it is still warm. A warm sponge is flexible; a cold one breaks. Lay a sheet of baking paper on the worktop, turn the hot sponge out onto it, peel off the lining paper, then roll it up inside the new paper. This sets the shape and stops it from drying out as it cools.

items.name: Hazelnuts
items.prepNote: blanched, lightly toasted and finely ground

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Line a Swiss roll tin (approximately 33 x 23 cm) with baking paper.
Whisk {{eggs}} yolks and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and thick, about 3 to 4 minutes with an electric mixer. The mixture should fall from the whisk in a thick ribbon.
Fold in {{hazelnuts}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} with a large spoon.
In a clean bowl, whisk egg whites to stiff peaks. Stir one large spoonful into the hazelnut mixture to loosen it, then fold in the rest in two additions, keeping as much air as possible.
Spread into the prepared tin. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until just set and lightly browned. The surface should spring back when pressed lightly.
While the sponge is baking, lay a clean sheet of baking paper on the worktop and dust it with icing sugar.

Turn the hot sponge out immediately onto the dusted paper. Carefully peel off the lining paper. Starting from one short end, roll the sponge up inside the paper. Leave to cool completely, seam side down.

Whip {{double-cream}} with {{icing-sugar}} and {{vanilla-extract}} to soft peaks. Unroll the cooled sponge gently. Spread the cream evenly over the inside, leaving a 2 cm border at the far short end. Re-roll tightly, using the paper to help. Trim the ends and transfer to a serving board.

attrs.intro: Roulades crack and sometimes split; this is normal.

items.fix: Roll while the sponge is still warm from the oven. An overbaked sponge becomes brittle and will crack badly; 18 to 20 minutes at the correct temperature is enough.
Twist the ends of the paper closed like a cracker and weigh the seam down with the tin while it cools.
items.symptom: Roulade unrolled itself when left to cool.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
